search_terms_used: genetic factors running performance endurance trainability research paper
trainability genetics endurance VO2max individual variation PubMed
individual response training variability non-responders endurance exercise
ACTN3 running performance genetics systematic review meta-analysis
HERITAGE Family Study VO2max trainability genetics Bouchard
Bouchard 2012 genomic predictors VO2max response standardized exercise HERITAGE
coverage_notes: Strong coverage across the domain. Includes 2 meta-analyses (ACTN3, ACE+ACTN3), 2 systematic reviews (marathon genetics, VO2max trainability genes), 1 GWAS (HERITAGE genomic predictors), 1 landmark family study (HERITAGE heritability), 1 RCT on dose-dependent non-response, and 3 narrative/comprehensive reviews covering non-responders, genetic testing utility, and overall gene-performance landscape. Populations are predominantly Caucasian/European with some African ancestry representation in HERITAGE. Most papers cover general endurance rather than running-specific populations, though 2 are specifically marathon/running-focused. Gap: limited coverage of epigenetics and gene-environment interaction mechanisms.

papers.title: Familial aggregation of VO(2max) response to exercise training: results from the HERITAGE Family Study
papers.notes: Examined familial aggregation and heritability of VO2max training response in 98 two-generation families undergoing 20 weeks of standardized cycle ergometer training.

papers.title: Genomic predictors of the maximal O2 uptake response to standardized exercise training programs
papers.notes: Conducted a GWAS on HERITAGE Family Study participants identifying 21 SNPs that accounted for 49% of variance in VO2max trainability, with replication in STRRIDE and DREW cohorts.

papers.title: The association of sport performance with ACE and ACTN3 genetic polymorphisms: a systematic review and meta-analysis
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of ACE and ACTN3 polymorphism studies examining associations between genotype and endurance vs power athletic performance.

papers.title: Genes to predict VO2max trainability: a systematic review
papers.notes: Systematic review identifying 97 genes associated with VO2max trainability across published intervention studies, noting limited replication and predominantly Caucasian samples.

papers.title: Refuting the myth of non-response to exercise training: 'non-responders' do respond to higher dose of training
papers.notes: RCT assigning 78 healthy adults to five different weekly exercise doses showing that non-response to endurance training is dose-dependent and disappears at higher training volumes.

papers.title: Do Non-Responders to Exercise Exist-and If So, What Should We Do About Them?
papers.notes: Narrative review examining whether true non-responders to exercise exist, concluding that global non-response is unlikely and alternative training modalities can rescue apparent non-responders.

papers.title: Can Genetic Testing Identify Talent for Sport?
papers.notes: Review examining whether genetic testing can predict future elite athletic performance, discussing current limitations and potential utility for guiding training and nutrition.

papers.title: Genes and Elite Marathon Running Performance: A Systematic Review
papers.journal: Journal of Sports Science and Medicine
papers.notes: Systematic review of 14 studies identifying 160 polymorphisms in 27 genes among marathon runners, finding 16 SNPs in 14 genes potentially associated with marathon performance.

papers.title: Biological and methodological factors affecting VO2max response variability to endurance training and the influence of exercise intensity prescription
papers.notes: Review examining biological factors (genetics, age, sex, baseline fitness) and methodological factors (training type, volume, intensity prescription) affecting VO2max response variability to endurance training.

papers.title: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of the Association Between ACTN3 R577X Genotypes and Performance in Endurance Versus Power Athletes and Non-athletes
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 25 studies across 13 countries examining ACTN3 R577X genotype distribution in power vs endurance athletes vs non-athletes.
